content: The half-life of Carbon-14 is approximately 5,730 years. This means that after 5,730 years, half of the original amount of Carbon-14 in a sample will have decayed into Nitrogen-14. This property makes Carbon-14 useful for radiocarbon dating archaeological and geological specimens.
raw_content: The half-life of Carbon-14 is approximately 5,730 years. This means that after 5,730 years, half of the original amount of Carbon-14 in a sample will have decayed into Nitrogen-14. This property makes Carbon-14 useful for radiocarbon dating archaeological and geological specimens.
